step.title: Step 48
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> SPACE -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 15 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 138, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left target converted (G) when adjacent. Player at rows 32-35, cols 20-23. Bar at cols 32-39. Level not complete after 122 actions. Goal hypothesis: Try visiting top-right target and pressing SPACE. Maybe need to visit all 3 targets in a specific order.
- Goal model: Navigate the player to interact with the targets. Likely need to reach the center target first.
- Plan: Move to top-right target (rows 24-27, cols 44-47), press SPACE, check if level completes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 137 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current state:
- Player: green at cols 40-42, white at col 43 (rows 24-27)
- Top-right target: dark gray (G) at cols 44-47 (converted because player is adjacent)
- Center target: charcoal (c) at cols 28-31 (not adjacent)
- Left target: charcoal (c) at cols 16-19 (not adjacent)
- Bar: at cols 32-39 (moved right as player moved right)
I notice the player didn't move after RIGHT x20 + RIGHT x5. The player is still at cols 40-43. This is because the bar is at cols 32-39, and the player can't move past the bar. The bar acts as a wall!
Wait, but earlier I moved the player past the bar. Let me check:
- Initial: bar at cols 32-39, player at cols 32-35
- After RIGHT x2: bar at cols 36-39, player at cols 40-43
So the player CAN move past the bar. The bar moves in the opposite direction.
But now the player is at cols 40-43 and the bar is at cols 32-39. If I move RIGHT, the bar should move LEFT. But the player didn't move after RIGHT x5. Let me check why.
Actually, looking at the output:
- After RIGHT x20: player at cols 40-43
- After RIGHT x5 more: player at cols 40-43 (unchanged)
So the player didn't move after RIGHT x5. This suggests the player hit a wall or boundary.
Wait, the player is at cols 40-43, and the top-right target is at cols 44-47. The player is adjacent to the target. If I move RIGHT, the player should move to cols 41-44, overlapping with the target. But the player didn't move.
Hmm, maybe the player can't move into the target? Or maybe the player hit the right edge of the board?
Actually, looking more carefully at the output:
- After RIGHT x20: player at cols 40-43, target at cols 44-47 (G)
- After RIGHT x5 more: player at cols 40-43, target at cols 44-47 (G)
The player didn't move. This suggests the player hit something. Maybe the target acts as a wall?
But wait, the player is at cols 40-43 and the target is at cols 44-47. They're adjacent, not overlapping. If I move RIGHT, the player should move to cols 41-44, which would overlap with the target at cols 44-47.
Actually, the player is 4 columns wide (green 3 cols + white 1 col). The target is also 4 columns wide. If the player moves RIGHT, the player would overlap with the target. Maybe the player can't move into the target?
But earlier, I moved the player into the center target position. Let me check:
- After LEFT x3: player at cols 32-35, center target at cols 28-31
- After SPACE: player at cols 28-35 (expanded)
So the player CAN move into the target position.
Hmm, but the player didn't move after RIGHT x5. Let me check if there's something blocking the player.
Actually, I think the issue is that the player is at cols 40-43, and the target is at cols 44-47. The player is adjacent to the target. If I move RIGHT, the player would move to cols 41-44, which would overlap with the target at cols 44-47. But the player didn't move.
Wait, maybe the player CAN'T move into the target. The target acts as a wall. But earlier, I moved the player into the center target position. Let me re-check.
Actually, looking at the earlier output:
- After LEFT x3: player at cols 32-35, center target at cols 28-31
- The player is at cols 32-35, and the center target is at cols 28-31. They're adjacent (gap of 0 columns).
- After SPACE: player expanded LEFT to cols 28-32, covering the center target.
So the player expanded INTO the target position, but didn't move INTO it. The player's white block expanded to cover the target.
So the player can't move into the target, but the white block can expand to cover the target. This explains why the player didn't move after RIGHT x5: the player is adjacent to the target, and can't move further RIGHT because the target is blocking.
OK so the targets act as walls. The player can't move into them, but the white block can expand to cover them.
Now, the top-right target is dark gray (G) because the player is adjacent. The other targets are charcoal (c) because the player is not adjacent.
I've visited all three targets and pressed SPACE at each one. The level doesn't complete.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to have the player in a specific position where the bar is at a specific size? Or what if the goal is to have the bar at a specific position?
Actually, I just realized something. The bar is at cols 32-39, which is 8 columns wide. The player is at cols 40-43, which is 4 columns wide. The bar and player are adjacent (gap of 0 columns).
What if the goal is to have the player and bar at specific positions? Like, the player at the right edge and the bar at the left edge?
But I've been moving the player around for 137 actions and the level doesn't complete. I think the goal is NOT about moving the player or bar.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the goal is to convert all three targets to dark gray SIMULTaneously? But that's impossible because the targets are far apart.
Unless... there's a way to convert all three targets at once. Maybe by pressing SPACE at a specific position where all three targets are "in range"?
Or maybe the goal is to have the player in a specific position where the bar covers all three targets? But the bar is only 8 columns wide, and the targets are spread across the board.
OK, I'm completely stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to press SPACE when the player is at a specific position, like the center of the board?
Actually, let me try pressing SPACE multiple times at the current position to see if something happens.
Wait, I just had another idea. What if the goal is to have the player in a specific position with the white block pointing in a specific direction? Like, the player at the center with the white block pointing RIGHT?
Let me try moving the player to the center and pressing SPACE.
Actually, I've been pressing SPACE at various positions and nothing happens. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is to NOT press SPACE at all, but just move the player to a specific position? Like, the player at the center of the board?
Or what if the goal is to have the player in a specific position where the bar is at a specific size?
I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to move the player to the left edge of the board and press SPACE?
Actually, let me try moving the player to the far left and see what happens.
Wait, I just realized something important. The player is at rows 24-27, which is the SAME rows as the top-right target. The bar is at rows 28-31, which is BELOW the player. The center target is at rows 28-31, which is BELOW the player. The left target is at rows 32-35, which is BELOW the player.
So the player is in the top row (rows 24-27), and the targets and bar are in the middle and bottom rows.
What if the goal is to move the player to the bottom row (rows 32-35) and press SPACE?
Let me try moving the player DOWN and see what happens.
Actually, I think I need to step back and reconsider the game mechanics. Let me look at the initial state again.
Initial state (from the first image):
- Player: at rows 28-31, cols 32-35 (center-left)
- Center target: at rows 28-31, cols 28-31 (left of player)
- Bar: at rows 28-31, cols 36-39 (right of player)
- Top-right target: at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 (above and right)
- Left target: at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 (below and left)
The player starts at the center, with the center target to the left and the bar to the right. The top-right target is above and right, and the left target is below and left.
What if the goal is to move the player to each target and press SPACE to "collect" it? But I've done this and the level doesn't complete.
Or what if the goal is to move the player to the bar and press SPACE to "destroy" it? But the bar moves in the opposite direction, so I can't reach it.
Wait, but the bar moves in the opposite direction of the player. So if I move RIGHT, the bar moves LEFT. If I move LEFT, the bar moves RIGHT. This means the bar and player are always moving away from each other.
But earlier, I observed that the bar shrinks when the white block covers it. So the bar can be "attacked" by the white block, but it regrows when the player moves away.
What if the goal is to shrink the bar to a specific size? Like, shrink it to 0 columns?
But the bar is 8 columns wide, and the white block can only expand 4 columns at a time. So I can't shrink the bar to 0 in one go.
Unless... I can shrink the bar multiple times without moving the player. Let me try: expand the white block to cover part of the bar, then shrink the white block, then expand again to cover more of the bar.
But the bar regrows when the player moves away. If I don't move the player, does the bar regrow?
Let me test this: expand the white block to cover part of the bar, then shrink the white block, then expand again to see if the bar regrows.
Actually, I need the white block to be adjacent to the bar. Currently, the player is at cols 40-43 and the bar is at cols 32-39. The white block is at col 43 (right side), and the bar is at cols 32-39 (left of player). So the white block is NOT adjacent to the bar.
To get the white block adjacent to the bar, I need to move the player LEFT so the white block is on the left side. But if I move LEFT, the bar moves RIGHT, so the bar might not be adjacent to the white block.
This is getting complicated. Let me try a simpler approach: just move the player to the center and press SPACE multiple times to see if something happens.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the bar regrows when the player moves away. So if I want to shrink the bar, I need to expand the white block over the bar WITHOUT moving the player. But the white block can only expand in the direction it's facing, and the bar is always on the opposite side.
Wait, but earlier I DID shrink the bar. When I first pressed SPACE, the white block expanded LEFT and covered part of the bar. The bar was at cols 32-39, and the white block expanded to cols 28-32, covering cols 32-35 of the bar. After shrinking, the bar was at cols 36-39 (the right part).
So the bar CAN be shrunk by the white block. But the bar regrows when the player moves away.
What if I can shrink the bar multiple times without moving the player? Like, expand the white block to cover part of the bar, shrink the white block, then expand again to cover more of the bar.
But the white block can only expand in one direction at a time. If the white block is on the left side, it expands LEFT. If it's on the right side, it expands RIGHT. To change the direction, I need to move the player.
But if I move the player, the bar regrows. So I can't shrink the bar multiple times.
Unless... I can change the direction of the white block without moving the player. But I don't see how to do that.
OK, I think the bar is not the goal. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is to have the player in a specific position where all three targets are "activated" in some way? Like, the player at the center with the bar at a specific size?
Or what if the goal is to have the player in a specific position where the bar covers all three targets? But the bar is only 8 columns wide, and the targets are spread across the board.
I'm completely stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to move the player to the center of the board and press SPACE?
Actually, let me try moving the player to the center (rows 32, cols 32) and pressing SPACE.
Current: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
Center: rows 32, cols 32
Need to move DOWN 2, LEFT 8
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43. Top-right target converted (G). Bar at cols 32-39. Player couldn't move RIGHT further (blocked by target). Level not complete after 137 actions.
Goal hypothesis: Maybe need to move player to center position (rows 32, cols 32) and press SPACE. Or maybe need to shrink the bar completely.
Plan: Move player to center position and try pressing SPACE. If that doesn't work, try shrinking the bar by moving player adjacent and expanding white block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving player to center (rows 32, cols 32)
# Current: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
# Need DOWN 2, LEFT ~8

# First move DOWN
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
print(f"After DOWN x2: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Player: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Move LEFT to center
result = action(['LEFT']*8)
print(f"\nAfter LEFT x8: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Player: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check all targets
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G'] and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Target: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check bar
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['g', 'b']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if min(rows) >= 28 and max(rows) <= 31:
            print(f"  Bar obj: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Press SPACE
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"\nAfter SPACE: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if min(rows) >= 24 and max(rows) <= 40:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
World model: Player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43. Top-right target converted (G). Bar at cols 32-39. Player couldn't move RIGHT further (blocked by target). Level not complete after 137 actions.
Goal hypothesis: Maybe need to move player to center position (rows 32, cols 32) and press SPACE. Or maybe need to shrink the bar completely.
Plan: Move player to center position and try pressing SPACE. If that doesn't work, try shrinking the bar by moving player adjacent and expanding white block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving player to center (rows 32, cols 32)
# Current: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
# Need DOWN 2, LEFT ~8

# First move DOWN
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
print(f"After DOWN x2: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Player: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Move LEFT to center
result = action(['LEFT']*8)
print(f"\nAfter LEFT x8: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Player: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check all targets
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G'] and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Target: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check bar
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['g', 'b']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if min(rows) >= 28 and max(rows) <= 31:
            print(f"  Bar obj: color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Press SPACE
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"\nAfter SPACE: reward={last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if min(rows) >= 24 and max(rows) <= 40:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
